perturbation.original_text: The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advisers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 3 above and this clause 4.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party reasonably secure and not to disclose it to any third party, provided that the Recipient has taken what it considers to be appropriate measures.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text in Section 4 replaces 'secure' with 'reasonably secure' and adds a subjective element ('provided that the Recipient has taken what it considers to be appropriate measures'). This creates a contradiction with clause 3, which strictly prohibits the Recipient from using the Confidential Information outside the Purpose, without any such qualifiers. Now, the standard of care for protecting the information (clause 4) is less rigorous than the prohibition on its unauthorized use (clause 3), making enforcement inconsistent, especially since the definition of 'appropriate measures' is left to the Recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient undertakes not to use the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party for any purpose except the Purpose, without first obtaining the written agreement of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4 replaces the 'secure' standard with a 'reasonably secure' standard, contingent on the Recipient's subjective assessment of 'appropriate measures.' This directly contradicts the strict prohibition in the contradicted text from Section 3, which requires written agreement for any use outside the defined 'Purpose.' The introduction of a reasonableness standard and Recipient's discretion undermines the absolute restriction outlined in the contradicted text, creating a clear and significant contradiction regarding the use and protection of Confidential Information. The Original Text in Section 4 provides clear guidelines on the use of Confidential Information to 3rd parties, while the Changed Text provides no such clarity. This change introduces ambiguity where the contradicted text expects certainty.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing in this Agreement will prevent the Recipient from making any disclosure of the Confidential Information required by law or by any competent authority.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing in this Agreement will prevent the Recipient from making any disclosure of the Confidential Information required by law or by any competent authority, provided that the Recipient notifies the other party within 30 days of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: While seemingly a minor addition, the 'provided that' clause in Section 6 introduces an obligation that clashes with the unconditional allowance for disclosures mandated by law or authority. Clause 5(a), which excludes information that 'comes into the public domain' from confidentiality obligations, now indirectly contradicts Section 6. If a legally mandated disclosure results in information entering the public domain without the 30-day notification (perhaps due to urgency or legal restrictions), it's unclear whether the Recipient is in breach. The conflict hinges on whether the notification requirement can be waived under exigent legal circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 above apply to all of the information disclosed by each of the parties to the other, regardless of the way or form in which it is disclosed or recorded but they do not apply to: a) any information which is or in future comes into the public domain (unless as a result of the breach of this Agreement); or b) any information which is already known to the Recipient and which was not subject to any obligation of confidence before it was dis closed to the Recipient by the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a 'provided that' clause requiring notification within 30 days for legally mandated disclosures. This contradicts the contradicted text, specifically clause (a), which states that information entering the public domain is not subject to confidentiality obligations if it does so without breach of the agreement. The contradiction arises because if a legal disclosure leads to information becoming public without the 30-day notification, it creates ambiguity as to whether a breach occurred, especially if there are legal restrictions that prevent the recipient from notifying the other party within the 30 day period. This creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will, on request from the other party, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party to the Recipient and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will, on request from the other party, make reasonable efforts to return all copies and records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party to the Recipient and will not intentionally retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification in Section 7, replacing 'return' with 'make reasonable efforts to return' and 'not retain' with 'not intentionally retain,' weakens the obligation to return confidential information. This undermines the Purpose clause (Section 1), which implies a complete return of all confidential information at the end of the defined period, to protect the confidentiality of the information. This is because it is now unclear whether the definition in section 1 still holds true, because the 'make reasonable efforts' undermines what is consider information to be confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each of the parties to this Agreement intends to disclose information (the Confidential Information) to the other party for the purpose of [insert details e.g. discussing the possibility of the parties entering into a joint venture] (the Purpose).
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the recipient 'will return all copies' and 'will not retain any copies'. The changed text modifies this to 'make reasonable efforts to return' and 'not intentionally retain'. This weakens the original obligation to return all confidential information, as 'reasonable efforts' and 'not intentionally' introduces ambiguity. This directly contradicts the purpose clause (section 1), which implies a complete return to protect confidentiality. The change undermines the clarity and enforceability of the confidentiality obligation, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither this Agreement nor the supply of any information grants the Recipient any licence, interest or right in respect of any intellectual property rights of the other party except the right to copy the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party solely for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither this Agreement nor the supply of any information grants the Recipient any licence, interest or right in respect of any intellectual property rights of the other party except the right to copy, modify, and create derivative works of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party solely for the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Section 8 now allows the Recipient to 'modify and create derivative works' of the confidential information. This is a direct contradiction of clause 4, which mandates keeping the information secure and not disclosing it. By allowing modification and derivative works, the modified information is no longer strictly the 'Confidential Information disclosed by the other party,' and its security is jeopardized. Modifying and creating derivative works increases the likelihood of disclosure, both accidental and intentional. There is an internal contradiction because section 4 specifies that the confidentiality of the Confidential Information has to be kept secure and not disclosed to any third party. Therefore, modifications of the Confidential Information is considered as a contradiction of the specified security clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advisers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 3 above and this clause 4.
perturbation.justification: The changed text expands the recipient's rights to not only copy confidential information but also to modify it and create derivative works. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which requires the recipient to keep the confidential information secure and not disclose it to any third party. Modifying the information inherently jeopardizes its security and increases the risk of disclosure, creating a strong and direct conflict between the two sections.
